skill.name: linear-claude-skill
skill.description: Manage Linear issues, projects, teams, and workflow status through MCP, CLI, and helper-script instructions.
skill.summary: Coordinate Linear issue and project workflows from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for command execution, network access, filesystem access, and environment variable handling are mostly true positives in context. The skill appears intended for legitimate Linear workflow automation, but it is a community Markdown skill that directs shell execution, API key setup, hidden-file writes, and remote MCP access without bundled scripts. No confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, but publication should wait for remediation and human review.

high_findings.title: Unbundled Relative Helper Script Execution
high_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs execution of relative helper scripts such as scripts/linear-ops.ts and scripts/linear-helpers.mjs, but the package contains only SKILL.md. In a user workspace, these commands could run unrelated project-local scripts or fail unpredictably.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and the local file structure shows no bundled helper scripts. The risk is semantic: relative script names resolve in the active workspace, not in the missing skill script directory.

high_findings.title: Credential and Hidden File Handling Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to configure LINEAR_API_KEY values, write to hidden Claude and shell files, and pass the key into MCP configuration. This is legitimate for Linear access but high risk if copied, logged, or modified incorrectly.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key and hidden-file references are directly present. The skill includes warnings against disclosure, which reduces evidence of malicious intent but does not remove credential-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the assistant to use Linear CLI, npx, node, varlock, and shell pipelines for normal operation. These commands can change Linear state and execute local tooling, so users need explicit trust and review before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are numerous and operational. They are aligned with the skill purpose, so this is not automatically malicious, but it is a real execution-risk surface.

medium_findings.title: Remote Linear and MCP Network Access
medium_findings.description: The skill directs access to Linear web resources and configuration of a remote MCP server at mcp.linear.app. Network access is expected for Linear, but it should be disclosed because issue data and metadata may leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are visible and relevant to Linear. The risk is moderate because the destinations appear product-related, not covert or unrelated.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm matches, but the reviewed lines are Linear workflow text, status values, or issue-management examples. No cryptographic operation was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence lines do not contain cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched substrings inside ordinary words or examples.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Low Context Risk
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged setup and validation instructions as reconnaissance. These checks are used to confirm Linear API key, SDK, CLI, and MCP availability, not to enumerate host secrets or exfiltrate system data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup checks are real, but the reviewed context is Linear tooling validation. No evidence found of broad host inventory collection.

low_findings.title: Self-Declared Safe Risk Metadata
low_findings.description: The skill front matter declares risk as safe. This must not be treated as authoritative marketplace evidence and should be replaced by independent review results.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The metadata is present and could bias a reviewer or loader. It is a weak signal because marketplace skill templates may include self-declared risk fields.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution with Credential Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines shell command execution with LINEAR_API_KEY setup and injection through environment variables. This combination increases the impact of command mistakes or malicious local scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combination is explicit across nearby setup and execution sections. The instructions also advise masking keys, so the pattern is risky but not confirmed malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative Script Paths in User Workspace
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands such as npx tsx scripts/linear-ops.ts use relative paths while no such scripts are included in the package. This can execute unexpected files from the current project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package contains only SKILL.md, and the relative script commands are repeated. That makes this a strong, evidence-backed unsafe pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Configuration File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guidance writes Linear API key material into hidden shell or Claude configuration locations. Hidden credential stores require clear permission boundaries and should not be modified implicitly by a skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write target and API key variable are explicit. The risk depends on user consent, but the security impact is clear.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent Linear updates without switching tools or losing project context. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for issues, projects, initiatives, labels, and status changes.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Linear MCP tools, Linear CLI, helper scripts, SDK scripts, or GraphQL.
Provides command patterns for creating issues, sub-issues, projects, and project links.
Documents status update workflows for issues and projects, including bulk status changes.
Gives setup guidance for LINEAR_API_KEY, Varlock checks, and MCP server configuration.
Defines project, initiative, status, and label conventions for consistent Linear organization.
limitations: The package contains only SKILL.md and does not include the referenced helper scripts.
It requires a valid Linear account and API key for most operations.
Some workflows depend on external tools such as npx, node, varlock, Linear CLI, or MCP.
It can change live Linear data, so users should review commands before execution.

use_cases.title: Plan a Product Phase
use_cases.description: Create a Linear project, link it to an initiative, and add structured parent issues and sub-issues.

use_cases.title: Sync Engineering Progress
use_cases.description: Update multiple issue statuses, apply label conventions, and mark project milestones after code changes.

use_cases.title: Operate Linear from Claude Code
use_cases.description: Use MCP or CLI workflows to inspect issues, add comments, and keep implementation tasks aligned.

prompt_templates.title: View an Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Linear skill to view issue ENG-123 and summarize its title, status, assignee, and next action.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Project Task Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Linear skill to create a project for the next release, link it to the right initiative, and draft five implementation issues.

prompt_templates.title: Bulk Update Statuses
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Linear skill to mark ENG-101, ENG-102, and ENG-103 as Done, then add a short completion comment to each issue.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Automation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Linear skill to choose between MCP, CLI, SDK, and GraphQL for a bulk project cleanup. Explain the safest execution plan first.

output_examples.input: Update ENG-123 to Done and add a short note.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the preferred Linear tool, confirms the issue target, updates the status, and records a concise completion comment.

output_examples.input: Create a new project for Phase 12 under Q2 Goals.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates the project, links it to the initiative, sets the planned state, and lists the first issues to create.

output_examples.input: Suggest labels for a security fix issue.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends one type label, one or two domain labels, and any relevant scope labels based on the documented taxonomy.

best_practices: Use MCP tools first when they are available and review any shell command before execution.
Keep LINEAR_API_KEY masked and use a secret manager or Varlock when possible.
Create projects and issues together so Linear reporting stays accurate from the start.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys into chat, terminal output, or issue comments.
Do not run referenced helper scripts unless their source and path are verified.
Do not create issues in a temporary holding project when the final project is known.

faq.question: Does this skill require Linear credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. Most operations require a Linear API key or an authenticated Linear MCP connection.

faq.question: Can it use Linear MCP tools?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill prefers Linear MCP tools when available and falls back to CLI or scripts.

faq.question: Can it update live Linear issues?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows can create issues, update status, add comments, and change projects.

faq.question: Are the helper scripts included?
faq.answer: No. This package contains only SKILL.md, so referenced helper scripts must be verified separately.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish without changes?
faq.answer: No. The audit recommends remediation because the skill directs shell execution and credential handling.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Users should review commands, script paths, environment variable handling, and the intended Linear workspace.
